data.title: Alliant Energy outlines 7%+ annual earnings growth target through 2029 as data center demand drives capital plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alliant Energy (LNT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Lisa Barton, President, CEO & Director, highlighted that "2025 was defined by major shifts in public policy, global trade, tax legislation and the acceleration of electric demand." She reported that ongoing 2025 EPS growth of 6% exceeded the midpoint of guidance and aligns with the long-term earnings growth target of 5 to 7-plus percent. Barton emphasized, "We also increased our dividend, marking the 22nd consecutive year of dividend increases and delivered a total shareholder return of over 13% for the year."
  • Barton detailed the completion of significant projects, including "275 megawatts of energy storage investments" and upgrades to the Neenah and Sheboygan Falls turbines, while noting proactive measures to safeguard planned renewable and storage projects against tax legislation changes. She described the strategic focus on data center growth, stating that in Iowa, "the approval of our first 2 individual customer rate contracts by the Iowa Utilities Commission provides a proven framework to support economic development while delivering benefits for all customers."
  • Barton announced the closure of the year with "4 executed ESAs totaling 3 gigawatts of peak load, translating to a 50% future growth in demand." She noted the relocation of the QTS data center project from Wisconsin to Iowa and confirmed a new electric service agreement for this project, asserting the company's capital expenditure program and investment growth expectations remain on track.
  • Barton stated that future growth is supported by "maximizing existing resources, extending asset life, investing in natural gas resources and strategically integrating renewables and energy storage facilities." Optimization and monetization of the fiber network was cited as a source of unique financial benefits.
  • Robert Durian, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "For the full year 2025, Alliant Energy delivered ongoing earnings per share growth of $0.18 compared to 2024." He cited drivers such as "increased revenue requirements from rate base increases, reflecting continued investment in generation and energy storage as well as favorable temperature impacts on electric and gas sales." Durian noted offsets from higher O&M expenses, increased depreciation, and financing expenses linked to expanding capital investments.
  • Durian highlighted a $0.05 charge from the suspension of production at Travero's wind turbine blade recycling operations and a $0.03 charge related to remeasurement of deferred tax assets. He confirmed the company's four-year capital plan remains on track, with flexibility to reallocate investments between Iowa and Wisconsin utilities following the QTS relocation.

Outlook

  • Durian affirmed 2026 earnings guidance and stated, "Our 2026 earnings guidance reflects several key assumptions. These include higher earnings from growing capital investments, including allowance for funds used during construction; expected retail sales growth of approximately 1%, inclusive of sales to new data centers during construction; higher O&M, depreciation and financing costs... and the ability to utilize investment tax credits from energy storage placed in service in 2025 and 2026 to support earning our authorized Iowa Electric ROE while maintaining stable base rates for our electric customers in Iowa."
  • Durian indicated, "We expect our compound annual earnings growth rate across 2027 to 2029 to be consistent with what we shared in November 2025, 7% plus."
  • Barton stated that Alliant is actively engaged with customers and "continue to pursue between 2 to 4 gigawatts of additional large load growth opportunities beyond what is already reflected in our current capital and financial outlook."

Financial Results

  • Durian reported that ongoing earnings for 2025 exclude two nonrecurring items: a $0.05 charge related to the suspension of Travero's wind turbine blade recycling operations and a $0.03 charge associated with remeasurement of deferred tax assets.
  • He specified that temperatures in 2025 contributed approximately $0.03 per share to electric and gas margins, compared to a reduction of approximately $0.15 per share in 2024.
  • Excluding temperatures, electric sales increased by nearly 1% in 2025 compared to 2024, driven by higher commercial and industrial sales.
  • The four-year capital plan remains at $13.4 billion, consistent with prior guidance, with about $2.4 billion of expected common equity needs over the period, of which about $1 billion has already been raised through forward equity agreements.
  • Durian detailed 2026 debt financing plans including up to $1.2 billion of long-term issuances: up to $400 million at the parent, up to $300 million at WPL, and up to $500 million at IPL.

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo Securities: Asked about minimum take agreements for the 3 gigawatts of data centers and their impact if ramped faster than plan. Barton responded, "Yes, that would absolutely be accretive to our planning assumptions."
  • Pourreza inquired about strategic focus shifting deals toward Iowa and safeguards against situations like the QTS move. Barton explained, "We've always talked about the fact that the differences between Iowa and Wisconsin... Iowa does have some strategic advantages."
  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: Asked about requirements for the relocated QTS site and tariff filings. Barton clarified, "We have the individual customer rate constructs that we use in both Wisconsin and Iowa... it's got a similar demand, similar timing, similar ramp rates, and they have land control and it is zoned industrial."
  • Campanella followed up on timing for new deals from the 2 to 4 gigawatts opportunity. Barton outlined, "We, in essence, have 3 buckets. We've got expansion opportunities at existing sites. We have existing customers in new locations, and we have new customers in new locations."
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked about land control and zoning for new projects. Barton confirmed, "All of that land is zoned industrial, if that's helpful."
  • Zimbardo asked about retail sales growth assumptions. Durian replied, "We are expecting to see some level of data center load start in the second half of 2026. But really, think about most of the load coming in from the data centers in '27 and beyond."
  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg: Inquired if CapEx shifts from gas to renewables were driven by QTS relocation. Durian stated, "Most of the recent shift... was just a shift between what we call gas generation to renewables."
  • Fremont asked about rate base growth and EPS growth rates. Durian noted, "We've built in some, I'll say, conservative assumptions as to what the new interest rates will be in the plan, and that's probably maybe a differentiating factor."
  • Andrew Weisel, Scotiabank: Asked if gas turbine and safe harbor credit reservations cover new expansion. Barton replied, "With respect to the 3 gigawatts, that is all in the plan... as we look towards the 2 to 4 gigawatts... we're also working on the generation side."
  • Weisel questioned CapEx timing changes. Durian said, "Most of those are pretty modest... just refinements to the process."
  • Rinny Singh, BofA: Asked about regulatory continuity amid upcoming races. Barton responded, "We actually posted recently this week on our website to ensure that there was clarity in terms of our philosophy, our commitment to our customers, which is -- they will not be paying for this data center growth."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally positive tone, focusing on growth opportunities, data center ramp, and regulatory positioning, while seeking clarity on timing and risk mitigation.
  • Management's sentiment during prepared remarks was confident, with Barton stating, "We are utilizing individual customer rates in both Iowa and with Wisconsin to ensure all customers benefit from economic development." During Q&A, management provided detailed, transparent responses and emphasized agility in pivoting strategy, without defensive or evasive language.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a positive and engaged tone, with analysts probing for detail on growth pipeline and regulatory assurances, and management reiterating confidence and flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 earnings and dividend was affirmed, maintaining the previously outlined targets. The company reiterated its 7%+ compound annual earnings growth rate through 2029, consistent with prior communications.
  • The most notable change was the relocation of the QTS data center project from Wisconsin to Iowa, followed by immediate execution of a new electric service agreement and the reallocation of capital investments, which management described as demonstrating agility and execution certainty.
  • Strategic focus remains on data center-driven growth, with an expanding pipeline of 2 to 4 gigawatts of additional opportunities, compared to the previous quarter's active pipeline and execution of a fourth ESA.
  • Management continued to express confidence in regulatory relationships and project execution, with no notable shift in tone from the previous quarter.
  • Analysts shifted focus slightly from project execution to questions about timing, regulatory approvals, and risk mitigation following the QTS relocation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified higher operating and maintenance expenses, increased depreciation, and financing expenses as partial offsets to earnings growth.
  • Two nonrecurring items impacted ongoing earnings: a $0.05 charge from the suspension of Travero's wind turbine blade recycling operations and a $0.03 charge for deferred tax asset remeasurement.
  • The company noted proactive actions to safeguard renewable and storage investments against evolving tax legislation.
  • Regulatory risk is mitigated by a lack of active rate reviews planned for 2026 and strong relationships with state regulators.
  • Analysts questioned safeguards to prevent project relocations like QTS, and management emphasized differences in state advantages and detailed due diligence for new ESAs.

Final Takeaway

Alliant Energy's 2025 results emphasized the company's ability to adapt quickly to shifting customer and market dynamics, highlighted by the seamless relocation and contracting of the QTS data center project. The company remains committed to delivering consistent earnings growth, supported by a robust capital plan and a pipeline of data center and large load opportunities. Management's focus on regulatory certainty, strategic flexibility, and disciplined execution, combined with proactive risk management, positions the company to drive shareholder value as it targets industry-leading earnings growth of 7% or more through 2029.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fed's Bostic says AI labor-market impact still an open question
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta President Raphael Bostic said Friday that business leaders in his district remain uncertain about whether AI will replace workers or simply enhance productivity.

Bostic, who is set to retire at the end of the month, noted that while AI is already reducing demand for certain entry-level positions, businesses are being “more cautious and patient” in their hiring as they try to understand the technology’s long-term impact.

Speaking in a moderated conversation with Stephanie Rebman, Birmingham Business Journal editor-in-chief, Bostic described a “real-time transformation” where businesses across the Southeast are actively experimenting with AI but lack clarity on its ultimate effect on their workforce needs.

“When we follow up with, ‘Are you sure what the impact is?’ Maybe a third of them say yes, but they’re still trying to figure this out,” Bostic said. “And then we follow that up with, ‘are you going to see this technology as replacing workers or supplementing their ability to do things?’ And nobody’s really clear on this.”

The Atlanta Fed president pointed to the coding profession as a stark example of how rapidly AI can disrupt established career paths.

“For the last 15 years, coders have been the hot place to be. But AI can actually code faster, so you don’t need as many people to be doing that,” Bostic explained. “And that happened in a three- or four-year period.”

Bostic connected the technological shift to broader labor market trends, noting that monthly job creation has fallen from over 100,000 positions a year ago to between 30,000 and 60,000 in recent months.

While such a decline would historically signal economic weakness, he suggested AI may be fundamentally changing the equation, as businesses find they can “produce the product without needing the extra people.” This represents what economists call a “structural change” that could permanently alter traditional business models.

For workers navigating this uncertain landscape, Bostic offered straightforward advice: adaptability is paramount.

“Perhaps the best skill and most important skill you can have is to be adaptable and to monitor the marketplace and try to get ahead of the curve,” he said, adding that the jobs of tomorrow may look nothing like those of even five years ago.

Despite the turbulence, Bostic said he remains “cautiously optimistic” about the U.S. economy, noting its remarkable resilience through recent challenges.

However, he acknowledged that AI, combined with inflationary pressures and geopolitical risks, has made the current period one of the most difficult of his tenure for policy forecasting.

“We are in a tremendous amount of flux, and it makes it very difficult,” he said.

More on the U.S. economy

  • 2025 real GDP growth was the softest in almost a decade, excluding COVID-19
  • U.S. GDP rises by 1.4% in Q4, below consensus, in BEA's advance estimate
  • Fears of inflation and collapsing growth ‘have not played out the way many thought’ – Jefferies’s David Zervos
  • Atlanta Fed GDPNow revises Q4 GDP growth downward to 3.0%
  • U.S. trade deficit in 2025 stands at $901B

data.title: OSB Group appoints CEO
data.description:
OSB Group (OSBGF) on Friday announced the appointment of Enrique Alvarez Labiano as CEO, effective from a date yet to be agreed.

Labiano is the CEO of retail and business banking and an executive director of the UK units of Banco Santander (SAN), Santander UK plc and Santander UK Group Holdings plc.

Labiano is set to succeed Andy Golding, who intends to retire.

data.title: Trump tariffs struck down—now comes the refund question
data.description:
With the U.S. Supreme Court's decision Friday to strike down President Trump's global tariffs has thrown open a big question for companies that have already paid the higher rates: will they get refunded?

The court's ruling held that Trump exceeded his authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act by imposing broad tariffs without clear congressional approval. But the opinion did not lay out how—or even whether—money already collected should be returned to importers.

More than 60% of 2025 tariffs "effectively vanish" after the SCOTUS decision, noted Olu Sonola, head of U.S. Economics at Fitch Ratings. "Mechanically, the U.S. effective tariff rate drops from about 13% to around 6%, removing more than $200 billion in expected annual tariff collections," he added. Similarly, Penn-Wharton Budget Model economists estimated that over $175B in government tariff revenue could be refunded. 

Dissenting Justice Brett Kavanaugh said in the ruling that "“the court says nothing today about whether, and if so how, the government should go about returning the billions of dollars that it has collected from importers. But that process is likely to be a ‘mess,’ as was acknowledged” during the court's oral arguments late last year. Beyond Kavanaugh's warning, SCOTUS didn't address the question of repayments, suggesting that any refunds will likely be handled by lower courts.

In any case, "the refund process will be a mess," said Heather Long. Chief Economist at Navy Federal Credit Union. "Small firms may struggle to get any money back from the U.S. Treasury." She added that the Trump administration will likely oppose refunds and may seek other potential avenues to carry out its key trade policy. 

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Reuters in an interview last week that the government has sufficient cash to repay revenue collected under IEEPA if ordered but warned that any refunds would likely be spread out over weeks or months and could extend beyond a year.

In a pre-ruling Goldman Sachs survey of over 200 investors, some 78% of respondents believed the court would block the “reciprocal” tariffs from 2025, though only 30% expected the ruling to result in refunds for previously paid duties.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Top 10 Oil And Gas Exploration and Production Stocks by Year-To-Date Performance
data.description:
Year to date, the energy sector has been one of the market’s strongest performers. In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 oil and gas exploration and production stocks ranked by their year-to-date performance as of February 20, 2026. 

The list is topped by Kosmos Energy (KOS), with a YTD performance of over 95%. Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and W&T Offshore (WTI) are next, with Venture Global (VG) and VAALCO Energy (EGY) rounding out the rest of the top five.

California Resources (CRC) and Northern Oil and Gas (NOG) also maintain strong double-digit YTD growth. Ovintiv (OVV), Permian Resources (PR), and Crescent Energy (CRGY) complete the top 10, each delivering gains above 26%.

Here is the list:

  1. Kosmos Energy (KOS), YTD perf: 95.06%, Quant Rating: 2.38

  2. Texas Pacific Land (TPL), YTD perf: 69.40%, Quant Rating: 3.00

  3. W&T Offshore (WTI), YTD perf: 60.74%, Quant Rating: 3.20

  4. Venture Global (VG), YTD perf: 45.01%, Quant Rating: 1.36

  5. VAALCO Energy (EGY), YTD perf: 41.21%, Quant Rating: 3.21

  6. California Resources (CRC), YTD perf: 32.86%, Quant Rating: 2.94

  7. Northern Oil and Gas (NOG), YTD perf: 29.76%, Quant Rating: 3.12

  8. Ovintiv (OVV), YTD perf: 29.29%, Quant Rating: 3.13

  9. Permian Resources (PR), YTD perf: 27.44%, Quant Rating: 3.17

  10. Crescent Energy (CRGY), YTD perf: 26.94%, Quant Rating: 3.04

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: Ardelyx's post-earnings decline attributed to concerns over Ibsrela outlook
data.description:
Ardelyx (ARDX) traded lower on Friday despite reporting better-than-expected Q4 2025 revenue, as Jefferies cited a softer-than-expected outlook for the company’s bowel disease therapy Ibsrela in the current quarter.

During the earnings call, Ardelyx (ARDX) CFO Sue Hohenleitner projected Ibsrela revenue growth in Q1 to follow a similar pattern to last year's period, a remark Jefferies analyst Dennis Ding said "implies an optically steep ramp.”

“Using 2025 as a proxy, we recorded approximately 16% of the full year IBSRELA revenues in the first quarter, and we anticipate that 2026 will likely follow a similar pattern,” Hohenleitner said.

Ding, who has a Buy rating on Ardelyx (ARDX), argued that management's remarks implied Ibsrela revenue for Q1 could reach ~$65M-$70M, which was below the $80M projected by Wall Street analysts.

For Q4 2025, the company recorded $86.6M in revenue from the small molecule therapy, indicating ~61% YoY growth, offsetting a ~51% YoY decline for its sister drug Xphozah, which added $27.8M to the topline.

Ardelyx (ARDX) set its full-year outlook for Xphozah revenue at $110M - $120M compared to $103.6M in 2025 and announced plans to achieve $1B in annual revenue from Ibsrela in 2029.

“We think guidance is doable + ARDX can still be profitable in H2," Bloomberg reported, quoting Jefferies' Ding. “ARDX remains controversial, and sentiment won’t change overnight, but we’re confident in management's strategy and reiterate it as our top pick,” he added.

data.title: Transocean outlines $11B pro forma backlog and targets 1.5x leverage post-Valaris acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Transocean Ltd. (RIG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Keelan Adamson, President and CEO, emphasized the company's strong operational and financial execution in 2025, highlighting "solid adjusted EBITDA of $385 million and free cash flow of $321 million" in the fourth quarter, with annual adjusted EBITDA of $1.37 billion and free cash flow of $626 million. Adamson stated, "During the year, we materially strengthened the balance sheet, retiring about $1.3 billion in debt...These actions and the additional debt payments made in 2025 reduced our annual interest expense by nearly $90 million, enhanced our financial flexibility and increased the value of our equity currency, ultimately enabling the recently announced transaction with Valaris."

  • Strategic cost reductions were detailed, with $100 million in costs removed and plans to decrease costs by an additional $150 million in 2026. Adamson noted, "We are leaner, more efficient and more profitable."

  • On the Valaris acquisition, Adamson described it as a "transformational combination" and stated, "We are incredibly excited about the capabilities of our combined business...Our pro forma combined backlog of nearly $11 billion and cash flow-generating capability are expected to accelerate debt reduction, resulting in leverage of around 1.5x within 24 months of closing."

  • Adamson provided a global market update, citing "tendering activity is increasing" and the expectation that "deepwater utilization to move meaningfully higher and to greater than 90% through 2027."

  • R. Vayda, Executive VP & CFO, added, "In the fourth quarter, we generated contract drilling revenues of $1.04 billion at an average daily revenue of approximately $461,000...Adjusted EBITDA was $385 million, implying a very healthy margin of 37% and cash flow from operations was approximately $349 million, a sequential increase of 42%."

Outlook

  • Management expects "free cash flow to be in line with or better than that achieved in 2025" for 2026, despite some anticipated idle time for several rigs, as ongoing cost and interest expense reductions and improved working capital management continue.

  • Vayda stated, "We expect to end 2026 with liquidity of between $1.6 billion and $1.7 billion, which excludes the effect of any incremental opportunistic deleveraging."

  • The company anticipates closing the Valaris transaction in the second half of 2026 and expects leverage to drop to "around 1.5x within 24 months of closing."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter contract drilling revenues were reported at $1.04 billion, with operating and maintenance expense at $605 million and G&A expense at $50 million.

  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $321 million, with a free cash flow margin of 31%. Liquidity at quarter end was approximately $1.5 billion, including $620 million in unrestricted cash and $510 million in undrawn credit.

  • The CFO noted, "This is the best quarterly free cash flow we have generated in several years and is a direct result of excellent operational performance, execution on our cost savings initiatives, lower cash interest expense and effective management of our working capital."

Q&A

  • Gregory Lewis, BTIG: Asked about post-acquisition chartering strategy and economies of scale. Adamson responded that the combination allows Transocean "to address the necessary cost across the combination...minimize...overlap of cost structure" and improve service provision globally, aiming for "reliable and predictable" execution.

  • Lewis also inquired about the jack-up market. Adamson indicated the company "learned from the past" and sees "a great opportunity for us to learn from the Valaris team that runs that jackup fleet to continue to deliver exceptional performance and incremental cash to the combined entity going forward."

  • Edward Kim, Barclays: Questioned confidence in a late 2026/2027 offshore inflection. Adamson cited customer conversations and "the number of tenders that release," while Roddie Mackenzie, Chief Commercial Officer, pointed to increasing rig awards and a growing number of multiyear programs.

  • Kim also asked about Petrobras blend and extend negotiations. Adamson confirmed guidance already reflects "our best guess based upon the conversations that we've had," with no significant upside assumed.

  • Fredrik Stene, Clarksons: Sought color on fleet placement and regional opportunities. Adamson and Mackenzie emphasized global flexibility and highlighted new tender activity, with Mackenzie noting "no shortage of opportunities" and potential for rigs to shift regions based on demand.

  • Doug Becker, Capital One: Asked about customer feedback on the Valaris deal. Adamson reported "overwhelmingly positive" response, stating customers "understand the need to drive costs out of the business."

  • Becker inquired about a win-win for Petrobras negotiations. Mackenzie explained the focus is on "cost reductions and optimization" and "significant extensions" for the core fleet, aiming to keep rigs busy and improve revenue efficiency.

  • Keith Beckmann, Pickering: Questioned the prospects for seventh-gen rigs and Gulf market assets. Adamson said reactivations would only occur if investment could be recovered, expressing confidence in long-term market strength but not expecting near-term reactivations.

  • Noel Parks, Tuohy Brothers: Asked about late entrants to offshore exploration. Mackenzie described a "pivot back towards oil and gas, particularly offshore and deepwater," with capital discipline driving focus on cost-effective, reliable energy.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with constructive inquiries about the Valaris acquisition, fleet utilization, and regional market opportunities. Questions were direct without evident skepticism or pressing negativity.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, particularly regarding cost savings, operational execution, and the strategic rationale for the Valaris acquisition. Phrases like "we are incredibly excited" and "we are well positioned to capitalize on improving demand" were prominent.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence was more pronounced, especially in context of the acquisition and strengthened balance sheet. Analysts’ tone remained consistently constructive, reflecting cautious optimism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured the major announcement of the Valaris acquisition, absent in the previous quarter.

  • Guidance language shifted to incorporate expectations from the pending merger and projected synergies, whereas the previous quarter focused on organic cost reductions and fleet rationalization.

  • Management’s tone increased in confidence, reflecting improved free cash flow, operational results, and strategic positioning.

  • Analysts’ focus broadened from utilization and day rates to include consolidation strategy, market opportunities, and synergies.

  • Key metric changes included higher adjusted EBITDA, free cash flow, and reported backlog post-acquisition announcement.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged "near-term moderation in tendering activity," but expects strengthening deepwater outlook.

  • Idle time risk for specific rigs (KG2, Deepwater Proteus, Deepwater Skyros) is reflected in guidance, with upside if contract extensions are secured.

  • Blend and extend negotiations with Petrobras remain a key variable, though management expects any impact is already captured in guidance.

  • The company is focused on "continued cost reduction initiatives" and maintaining financial flexibility to manage industry cycles.

Final Takeaway

Transocean’s management underscored a transformative year marked by operational excellence, aggressive deleveraging, and a pending acquisition that will create a combined entity with nearly $11 billion in backlog. The leadership team is projecting continued strong free cash flow, significant cost reductions, and accelerated debt reduction, targeting leverage of 1.5x within two years of closing the Valaris deal. With a constructive market view underpinned by rising tendering activity and a robust pipeline of multiyear offshore projects, the company positions itself as a leading, resilient player in the evolving offshore drilling sector.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
